text: The laparoscopic view shows normal pelvic anatomy. A brisket attractor is placed in the vagina, and dissection is performed into the rectovaginal space, reaching the level of the rectovaginal septum.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter and the sacral promontory. An incision is made in the peritoneum over the sacral promontory, extending down towards the recto-vaginal space.

The surgeon performs dissection into the presacral space, exposing the anterior longitudinal ligament of the sacrum. A 4x17 cm strip of polypropylene mesh is introduced and sutured to the iliac oxygen muscle bilaterally using two OPDS sutures.
The surgeon performs the procedure on the contralateral side, mirroring the previous steps of mesh placement and suturing to the anterior vaginal wall.
An EEA sizer is inserted into the rectum, and the surgeon sutures the polypropylene mesh to the anterior rectal wall using four interrupted 2-0 PDS sutures.

The EEA sizer delineates the rectal wall, visible through a ten millimeter camera port at the umbilicus. An eight millimeter port on the right side is used for introducing sutures and tying knots extracorporeally with a closed end knot pusher. Additional five millimeter ports are located on the right and left sides.
The surgeon dissects into the vesicle vaginal space, with the bladder visibly distended by 200 cc of water to clearly delineate its edge.

A second strip of polypropylene mesh, measuring four by twelve centimeters, is introduced into the abdominal cavity and sutured to the anterior vaginal wall using five to six interrupted sutures of 2-0 PDS.

The surgeon tensions two strips of polypropylene mesh against the anterior longitudinal ligament of the sacrum and secures them with two zero-proline sutures. Excess mesh is trimmed before placing the second stitch.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal incision using 2-0 monocryl sutures in a purse-string fashion, effectively retroperitonealizing the mesh within the abdominal cavity.
